content.full_text:
1/ 
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
428 ,J Street• Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329 
(916) 322-5660 • Fox (916) 322-0886 
Amy R. Webber 
Deputy City Attorney 
Long Beach City Attorney's Office 
333 West Ocean Blvd., 11th Floor 
Long Beach, CA 90802-4664 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-16-007 
Dear Ms. Webber: 
February 25, 2016 
This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict of interest provisions of 
the Political Reform Act (the "Act")1 and Section 1090. The Fair Political Practices Commission 
(the "Commission") is not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC 
Ops. 71 ), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. Please note that 
we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the Act and Section 1090, 
not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of interest. 
When a request for advice involves potential issues raised under Section 1090, we are required to 
forward a copy of the request to the Attorney General's Office and the local district attorney prior to 
proceeding with the advice. (Section 1097.l(c)(3).) Accordingly, we have forwarded your request 
to the Attorney General's Office and the Los Angeles County District Attorney's Office. We did 
not receive a written response from either entity. (Section 1097.l(c)(4).) The following advice is not 
admissible in a criminal proceeding against any individual other than the requestor. 
(Section 1097.l(c)(S).) 
QUESTIONS 
I. Does the Act prohibit the Long Beach Police Department (the "Department") from 
purchasing the right to use a community outreach program (the "Program") from a non-profit that 
acquired the Program from Police Officer Jason Lehman, an employee of the Department, who 
developed the Program and serves as the non-profit's uncompensated Executive Director? 
2. Does Section 1090 prohibit the Department from purchasing the right to use the 
Program? 
3. Does Section 1090 prohibit the Department from using the Program if the non-profit 
gives the Department the right to use the Program? 
1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 8 IO00 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 


File No. A-16-007 
Page No. 2 
4. If the Department acquires the right to use the Program, does Section 1090 
prohibit Officer Lehman from using the Program as part of his assigned duties? 
CONCLUSIONS 
I. No. Under the Act, Officer Lehman does not have a financial interest in the Program. 
2-4. No. Under Section I 090, Officer Lehman is not making or participate in making a 
contract for the purchase of the right to use the Program while acting in his official capacity, as 
discussed below. 
FACTS 
While off-duty, Officer Lehman developed a concept for the Program, a community 
outreach program focusing on youth interactions with police. The Program, called "Why'd You 
Stop Me?, was developed to infonn inner city youths through community outreach about police 
procedures and police interactions within the community. The Program's goal is to reduce violence 
between the community and police by having both parties step into each other's shoes. Officer 
Lehman created the materials and made presentations at local high schools and community groups 
on his own time and at no charge. He did not wear his police uniform at these presentations. 
The Program became popular and Officer Lehman created a non-profit corporation to 
support the Program. He then donated to the non-profit any ownership rights he had in the Program. 
The non-profit has an eight-member board of directors, which includes a team of community 
leaders. Officer Lehman serves without compensation as the non-profit's Executive Director. 
The Department would like to acquire the right to use the Program in its training and 
community outreach. 
ANALYSIS 
Co11jlict of lllterest 1111der tl,e Act 
Under Section 87103 and Regulations 18703 - 18703.5, inclusive, a public official has a 
financial interest in: 
• A business entity in which he or she has a direct or indirect investment of $2,000 or 
more (Section 87103(a)) or in which he or she is a director, officer. partner. trustee. 
employee, or holds any position of management. (Section 87103( d).) 
• Real property in which he or she has a direct or indirect interest of $2,000 or more. 
(Section 87103(b).) 
• Any source of income, including promised income, totaling $500 or more within 12 
months prior to the decision. (Section 87103(c.)) 


File No. A-16-007 
Page No. 3 
• Any source of gifts to him or her if the gifts total $460 or more within 12 months prior to 
the decision. (Section 87103(e).) 
• His or her personal expenses, income, assets, or liabilities, as well as those of his or her 
immediate family. This is commonly referred to as the "personal financial effects" rule. 
(Section 8 7103.) 
Business Entity 
If a public official is a director, officer. patiner, trustee. employee. or holds any position of 
management in a business. the official has a financial interest in the business entity. However. 
under Section 82005, a non-profit organization is not a "business entity.'· Therefore, Officer 
Lehman's position as the Executive Director of the non-profit docs not give rise to a financial 
interest in a business entity. 
Source of Income & Personal Finances 
You state that Officer Lehman receives no compensation from the non-profit. Therefore, the 
non-profit is not a source of income to him nor does the Department's decision to purchase rights to 
the Program afect his personal finances. Accordingly, under the Act's conflict of interest 
provisions, Officer Lehman docs not have a financial interest in the decision.2 
Co11jlict of /11terest 1111der Sectio11 1090 (Q11estio11s 2, 3 &4) 
Section 1090 generally prohibits public officers, while acting in their official capacities, 
from making contracts in which they are financially interested. We normally employ a six-step 
analysis to determine whether an official has a conflict of interest under Section I 090, but we need 
only address the first three steps, as explained below. 
Step One: Is the official subject to the provisions of Section 1090? 
Section I 090 provides, in part, that "[m]embers of the Legislature, state, county, district, 
judicial district, and city officers or employees shall not be financially interested in any contract 
made by them in their official capacity, or by any body or board of which they are members.'' As a 
city employee, Officer Lehman is subject to the provisions of Section I 090. 
Step Two: Does the decision at issue involve a contract? 
To determine whether a contract is involved in the decision, one may look to general 
principles of contract law (84 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 34, 36 (2001 ); 78 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 230, 234 
(1995)), while keeping in mind that "specific rules applicable to Sections I 090 and 1097 require 
that we view the transactions in a broad manner and avoid narrow and technical definitions of 
2 Please note that our conclusion is based on additional information you provided after submitting your request 
for advice indicating that Officer Lehman no longer owns the Program and that at any purchase agreement would be 
made between the Department and the non-profit. If Officer Lehman still owned the Program or received any income 
from the non-profit, the result would be different 


File No. A-16-007 
PageNo.4 
'contract."' (People v. Honig ( 1996) 48 Cal.App.4th 289, 351) citing Stigall v. Taft (1962) 58 Cal.2d 
565. 
Question 2. Under general principles oflaw, a contract is made on the mutual assent of the 
parties and consideration. If the Department agrees to purchase the right to use the Program, there is 
mutual assent by the parties and consideration. Therefore, the decision to purchase the rights 
involves a contract. 
Question 3. As stated above, a basic element of a contract is consideration. If the non-profit 
permits the Department to use the Program without receiving any compensation, or other 
consideration, there is no contract. 
Q11estio11 4. After the Department acquires the use of the Program, Officer Lehman's use of 
the Program as part of his duties does not involve a contract. 
Thus, we proceed to analyze question 2 because that is the only question that involves a 
contract. 
Step Three: Will the official be making or participating in making a contract? (Question 2) 
As stated above, a contract is "made" on mutual assent of the involved parties. (Stigall, 
supra, at p. 569.) In addition, making or participating in making a contract has been broadly 
construed to include those instances where a public official has influence over the contract or its 
terms. (See 80 Ops. Cal. Atty. Gen. 41 (1997).) No contract is "made" and no duties attach until one 
party has offered and another has accepted and consideration is exchanged. But before this, there 
are "negotiations, discussions, reasoning, planning, and give and take," all of which are 
instrumental in "making" the agreement. (Stigall, supra, at p. 569.) 
However, Section 1090 prohibits employees from making or participating in making 
contracts in which they are financially interested only while acting in their official capacities.3 The 
critical question, therefore, is whether Officer Lehman was acting in his official capacity when he 
was discussing the Program and the potential for the Department's use of the Program with 
Department staff. 
In 80 Ops. Cal.Atty. Gen. 41 ( 1997), two city firefighters were permitted to sell a firefighting 
protective device, which they invented in their private capacity, to their fire department so long as 
they played no role whatsoever in their official capacity in the department's contracting process. 
Consistent with this opinion, so long as Officer Lehman has no input in the Department's 
decision to purchase the right to use the Program while acting in his official capacity, he would not 
be making or participating in making a contract. 
3 In contrast, participation is conclusively presumed for members of an agency's governing board irrespective 
of whether a board member actually participates in the making of the contract. (Thomson v. Call (1985) 38 Cal.3d 633, 
645 & 649.) 


File No. A-16-007 
Page No. 5 
Accordingly, under the facts you have provided, the Department is not prohibited by Section 
1090 from entering into a contract for the purchase of the right to use the Program. 
VJ:jgl 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
Sincerely, 
Hyla P. Wagner 
General Counsel 
By: 
Valentina Joyce 
Counsel, Legal Division

sections.question:
I. Does the Act prohibit the Long Beach Police Department (the "Department") from

acquired the Program from Police Officer Jason Lehman, an employee of the Department, who

3. Does Section 1090 prohibit the Department from using the Program if the non-profit 
gives the Department the right to use the Program?  
4. If the Department acquires the right to use the Program, does Section 1090 
prohibit Officer Lehman from using the Program as part of his assigned duties?

I. No. Under the Act, Officer Lehman does not have a financial interest in the Program. 
2-4. No. Under Section I 090, Officer Lehman is not making or participate in making a 
contract for the purchase of the right to use the Program while acting in his official capacity, as 
discussed below.

acquired the Program from Police Officer Jason Lehman, an employee of the Department, who 
developed the Program and serves as the non-profit's uncompensated Executive Director? 
2. Does Section 1090 prohibit the Department from purchasing the right to use the 
Program? 
3. Does Section 1090 prohibit the Department from using the Program if the non-profit 
gives the Department the right to use the Program?  
4. If the Department acquires the right to use the Program, does Section 1090 
prohibit Officer Lehman from using the Program as part of his assigned duties?

CONCLUSION: I. No. Under the Act, Officer Lehman does not have a financial interest in the Program. 
2-4. No. Under Section I 090, Officer Lehman is not making or participate in making a 
contract for the purchase of the right to use the Program while acting in his official capacity, as 
discussed below.
